topic_description: Visualize the average time between a track’s release and royalty payment to rights-holders across major music distributors and collection societies, comparing 2018 vs 2024.

master_data.metric_name_primary: Average royalty payout delay from release to payment

statistical_properties.trend_description: Bimodal delays: faster cycles among distributors/aggregators (roughly 28–74 days) and slower settlement among collection societies (roughly 88–102 days). Negative relationship where higher payout frequency corresponds to shorter delays; PRS for Music and BMI/PRS sit as high-delay outliers versus DSP/distributor cluster.

bar.ground_truth_caption: The bar chart compares the average royalty payout delay (days from release to payment) across music distributors and collection societies in 2018. PRS for Music has the longest delay at 102 days, followed by ASCAP at 96 days and BMI at 88 days, while DistroKid is the fastest at 28 days (TuneCore 33 and TikTok Music 39). Major streaming services fall mid-range, such as Spotify at 52 days and Apple Music at 45 days, with Deezer relatively high at 74 days. Overall, payout delays range widely from 28 to 102 days, with collection societies generally slower than distributors.

scatter.ground_truth_caption: The scatter plot “Payout Frequency vs Average Royalty Payout Delay (2018)” compares music platforms and rights organizations by payout frequency (payments/year) and average delay from release to payment (days). PRS for Music has the longest delay at 102 days with the lowest frequency (5/year), followed by ASCAP at 96 days (6/year) and BMI at 88 days (7/year). The shortest delays occur for DistroKid (28 days, 12/year) and TikTok Music (39 days, 46/year), while major streaming services cluster at higher frequencies (28–42/year) with mid-range delays (45–74 days). Overall, higher payout frequency generally corresponds to shorter payout delays.

pie.ground_truth_caption: The pie chart titled "Share of Total Average Royalty Payout Delay by Entity (2018)" compares average royalty payout delays (days) across music distributors and collection societies. PRS for Music has the largest delay at 102 days, followed by ASCAP at 96 days and BMI at 88 days. Mid-range delays include Deezer (74 days), YouTube Music (67), and Amazon Music (58), while the shortest delays are DistroKid (28 days) and TuneCore (33 days). Overall, the distribution is skewed toward longer delays among the collection societies compared with most distributors.

Overall, the points show a clear positive relationship: longer travel distances correspond to higher per-show emissions.

pie.ground_truth_caption: The pie chart shows the share of estimated CO2e per show across 10 K-pop world tours from 2022–2024. pie.ground_truth_caption: The pie chart shows the average share of new audiobook releases by narration style from 2016–2024. statistical_properties.trend_description: Ranked first-week sales show a sharp drop from the top release and then taper off; higher first-week sellers tend to have higher week-1 share. Days to reach 100k generally increase as first-week sales decline.

bar.y_label: First-week album sales (units (first-week physical sales))
bar.img_title: Top K-pop Releases by First-week Album Sales (2012)

scatter.x_label: Week-1 sales share of first-month total (% (week-1 share of first-month total))
scatter.y_label: First-week album sales (units (first-week physical sales))
scatter.img_title: First-week Album Sales vs Week-1 Share of First-month Total (2012)

pie.img_title: Share of Total First-week Album Sales by K-pop Release (2012)

bar.ground_truth_caption: The bar chart titled "Top K-pop Releases by First-week Album Sales (2012)" ranks 10 K-pop releases by first-week physical album sales. Super Junior leads with 35,240 units, followed by BIGBANG at 28,710 and Girls' Generation at 24,180, with SHINee (19,860) and TVXQ (17,110) next. Sales then decline through 2NE1 (13,290), Beast/Highlight (10,940), and CNBLUE (8,620), with the lowest totals for T-ara (6,410) and KARA (5,120). Overall, the distribution shows a steady drop from the top seller to the bottom.

scatter.ground_truth_caption: The 2012 scatter plot compares K-pop groups’ first-week physical album sales (y-axis) with the percentage of first-month sales coming from week 1 (x-axis). Super Junior has the highest first-week sales at 35,240 units with a 46.8% week-1 share, while BIGBANG shows the highest week-1 share at 52.3% and 28,710 units; Girls’ Generation follows at 24,180 units and 44.1%. At the low end, KARA records 5,120 units at 27.9% and T-ara 6,410 at 29.4%. Overall, higher week-1 shares tend to align with higher first-week sales, though the relationship is not perfectly linear.

pie.ground_truth_caption: The pie chart shows the share of total first-week album sales across 10 K-pop releases in 2012. Super Junior holds the largest portion with 35,240 first-week sales, followed by BIGBANG at 28,710 and Girls’ Generation at 24,180. Mid-range shares include SHINee (19,860) and TVXQ (17,110), while the smallest slices are T-ara (6,410) and KARA (5,120). Overall, the distribution is top-heavy, with the leading few acts accounting for a large fraction of total sales.

topic_description: Visualize user retention and contribution activity across major fan fiction and transformative works platforms over a recent 12-month cohort window.

master_data.unit_secondary: chapters per active author per month (avg, 1 decimal)
master_data.metric_name_secondary: Average chapters posted per active author per month

statistical_properties.trend_description: Two retention clusters: higher-retention community/forum-anchored platforms (~46–50%) vs lower-to-mid discovery/legacy platforms (~22–36%). Posting intensity tends to rise with retention but remains noisy (serial-fiction-heavy sites show higher chapters/month).

bar.y_label: 30-day cohort retention rate (% (30-day cohort retention))
bar.img_title: 30-day Cohort Retention Rate by Fan Fiction Platform (2025 Rolling 12-month Cohorts)

scatter.x_label: Average chapters posted per active author per month (chapters per active author per month (avg, 1 decimal))
scatter.y_label: 30-day cohort retention rate (% (30-day cohort retention))
scatter.img_title: Retention vs Posting Intensity Across Fan Fiction Platforms (2025 Rolling 12-month Cohorts)

pie.img_title: Distribution of 30-day Cohort Retention Rate Across Fan Fiction Platforms (2025 Rolling 12-month Cohorts)

bar.ground_truth_caption: The bar chart compares 30-day cohort retention rates (%) across fan fiction platforms for 2025 rolling 12-month cohorts. SpaceBattles Creative Writing has the highest retention at 49.6%, followed by Sufficient Velocity Creative Writing at 47.8% and Archive of Our Own (AO3) at 46.2%. The lowest retention is on FanFiction.net (22.5%), with Asianfanfics (26.9%) and Webnovel (28.7%) also trailing. Overall, most platforms cluster in the mid-20s to high-40s, with the two forum-based communities leading.

scatter.ground_truth_caption: The scatter plot compares 30-day cohort retention (%) against average chapters posted per active author per month across 10 fan fiction platforms (2025 rolling 12-month cohorts). The highest retention is for SpaceBattles Creative Writing at 49.6% (6.2 chapters/month), followed by Sufficient Velocity Creative Writing at 47.8% (5.7) and AO3 at 46.2% (3.1). The lowest retention is FanFiction.net at 22.5% (2.4), while the highest posting intensity is Webnovel at 10.7 chapters/month but with relatively low retention (28.7%). Overall, retention tends to be higher in the mid-range posting intensity (~5–6 chapters/month) rather than at the extremes.

pie.ground_truth_caption: The pie chart compares 30-day cohort retention rate values across 10 fan fiction platforms for 2025 rolling 12-month cohorts. SpaceBattles Creative Writing has the highest retention at 49.6%, followed by Sufficient Velocity Creative Writing at 47.8% and Archive of Our Own (AO3) at 46.2%. Mid-range values include Royal Road (40.9%) and Scribble Hub (36.1%), while the lowest retention is FanFiction.net at 22.5% (with Asianfanfics at 26.9% and Webnovel at 28.7%). Overall, retention values are broadly spread from the low 20s to just under 50%.
